criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the visual tokenizer pipeline (encoder, quantizer, decoder) and the autoregressive model formulation.
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the two sources of generator-tokenizer inconsistency: amplified exposure bias and embedding unawareness.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the two components of reAR: Noisy Context Regularization and Codebook Embedding Regularization.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the experimental setup including dataset, evaluation protocol, model configurations, and training details.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the main class-conditional generation results on ImageNet-1K 256×256.
The agent has read Table 2 to check generalization results on non-standard tokenizers (TiTok and AliTok).
The agent has read Tables 3 and 4 to check the ablation study settings for noise augmentation and embedding regularization layer selection.

The agent has read Appendix A completely to understand additional model configuration details (VQGAN codebook size 1024, 16×16 patches, QK-Norm, AdaLN, dropout 0.1, MLP ratio 4.0, 16 attention heads) and training details (β1=0.9, β2=0.96, weight decay=0.03, bfloat16).
The agent has read Appendix B to understand the analysis experiment settings for studying generator-tokenizer inconsistency (CTR, perplexity, PSNR, LPIPS metrics).
The agent has read Appendix C to understand the CKA-based analysis of hidden feature alignment between the AR generator and the tokenizer.

The plan includes implementing the amplified exposure bias analysis with two decoding protocols: perfect context (front-loaded ground-truth tokens) and imperfect context (uniformly interleaved ground-truth tokens).
The plan includes implementing the embedding unawareness analysis experiment by replacing incorrect token predictions with tokens whose embedding is closest in cosine similarity to the correct token.
The plan includes the Noisy Context Regularization component: injecting Bernoulli noise into input tokens during training using the annealing schedule.

The plan includes the Codebook Embedding Regularization component: adding trainable MLP heads at a shallow layer (encoding) and a deep layer (decoding) to predict current and next token codebook embeddings respectively.
The plan specifies the combined reAR objective combining the noisy context AR loss and the codebook embedding regularization loss with weight λ.
The plan specifies the MLP head architecture: 2-layer MLP with hidden size 2048 and GeLU activation, with parameter overhead of 2.1M/2.1M/4.2M for reAR-S/B/L.
The plan specifies placing the encoding regularization at layer l=0 (first transformer layer) and the decoding regularization at l'=15/18/18 for reAR-S/B/L (three-quarters model depth).
The plan specifies the truncated linear annealing noise schedule f(t) = max(0, 1 - 4/3 * t) for sampling ε ~ U(0, f(t)) during training.
The plan includes model configurations for reAR-S (20 layers, hidden size 768, 201M params), reAR-B (24 layers, hidden size 768, 261M params), and reAR-L (24 layers, hidden size 1024, 461M params).
The plan includes the training setup: 400 epochs, 8 A800 GPUs, batch size 2048, AdamW optimizer with gradient clipping, learning rate warmup followed by cosine decay.
The plan includes classifier-free guidance with class label dropout probability 0.1 during training and power-cosine guidance schedule at inference.
The plan includes ablation experiments for noise augmentation strategies (fixed ratio vs. uniform sampling vs. annealing schedules).
The plan includes ablation experiments for embedding regularization layer selection, testing encoding and decoding regularization at different transformer layers.
The plan includes generalization experiments applying reAR to TiTok (AR-TiTok-b64, 261M params, 400 epochs) and AliTok (reAR-B-AliTok, 177M params, 800 epochs) tokenizers.
The plan includes applying reAR to VAR-d16 using the same training settings to validate generalization to non-standard AR paradigms.

The code for the visual tokenizer pipeline has been implemented following Equation 1: encoder E maps image I to feature z-hat, quantizer Q maps to quantized embedding z^q, and decoder D reconstructs the image.
The code for the MaskGIT VQGAN tokenizer has been integrated with a codebook of size 1024 producing 16×16 patch features.

The code for the DiT-style decoder-only AR backbone has been implemented with causal mask, class conditioning via AdaLN layers, learnable positional embeddings, QK-Norm, dropout 0.1, and MLP ratio 4.0 with 16 attention heads.
The code for the standard autoregressive training objective has been implemented as Equation 3: maximizing log-likelihood of next token given ground-truth context (teacher forcing).

The code for the Noisy Context injection has been implemented as Equation 4: each input token x_i is replaced with a uniform random codebook index u_i with probability ε (Bernoulli(ε)), i.e., x-tilde_i = (1 - b_i) * x_i + b_i * u_i.
The code for the modified AR loss under noisy context has been implemented as Equation 5: L_AR'(θ) = -E[sum log p_θ(x_i | x-tilde_{i-1}, ..., x-tilde_1)] where ε ~ U(0, f(t)) is sampled per sequence.
The code for the 2-layer MLP head h_φ has been implemented with hidden size 2048 and GeLU activation to project transformer hidden features into the tokenizer codebook embedding dimension.

The code for the Codebook Embedding Regularization loss has been implemented as Equation 6: L_re(θ,φ;t) = E[sum d(h_φ^i(w_θ^l(x-tilde)), z_{x_i}) + d(h_φ^i(w_θ^{l'}(x-tilde)), z_{x_{i+1}})] using cosine distance.
The code for the combined reAR training objective has been implemented as Equation 7: L_reAR(θ,φ;t) = L_AR'(θ;t) + λ * L_re(θ,φ;t) with default λ=1.
The encoding regularization is applied at transformer layer l=0 (first layer) to align early hidden features with the current token's codebook embedding z_{x_i}.

The decoding regularization is applied at transformer layer l'=15/18/18 for reAR-S/B/L respectively (three-quarters model depth) to align deep hidden features with the next token's codebook embedding z_{x_{i+1}}.
The annealing noise schedule f(t) = max(0, 1 - 4/3 * t) has been implemented, where t is the normalized training progress (e.g., t=3/8 at epoch 150 of 400).
The AdamW optimizer has been configured with β1=0.9, β2=0.96, weight decay=0.03, gradient clipping norm=1.0, and bfloat16 mixed precision training.
The learning rate schedule has been implemented: linear warmup to 4×10^{-4} over the first 100 epochs, followed by cosine decay to 1×10^{-5} over the remaining 300 epochs.
Gradient accumulation has been implemented: 1 step for reAR-S/B and 2 steps with batch size 1024 per step for reAR-L to achieve effective batch size 2048.

Classifier-free guidance has been implemented: class labels dropped with probability 0.1 during training, and power-cosine sampling schedule applied at inference with guidance scale 22/14.5/10 and power scale 2.75/2.25/1.75 for reAR-S/B/L.
KV-cache has been implemented for autoregressive inference to achieve high sampling throughput.
FID evaluation has been implemented following the ADM protocol: generating 50,000 images at 256×256 and computing FID against the ImageNet-1K validation split.
Inception Score (IS) evaluation has been implemented following Salimans et al. (2016) for the 50,000 generated images.
The correct token ratio (CTR) metric has been implemented as Equation 8: CTR = (1/N) * sum_i 1[argmax_v p_θ(v|x_{1:i-1}) = x_i].
The perplexity metric has been implemented as Equation 9: Perplexity = exp(-(1/N) * sum_i log p_θ(x_i | x_{1:i-1})).
The PSNR metric has been implemented as Equations 10-11: MSE between decoded and ground-truth images, then PSNR = 10 * log10(L^2 / MSE).
The LPIPS metric has been implemented as Equation 12: weighted sum of squared differences between pretrained network activations of decoded and ground-truth images.
The Perfect Context (front-loaded) decoding protocol has been implemented: fixing the first floor(r*n) tokens to ground truth and letting the AR model freely generate the remainder.

The Imperfect Context (uniformly interleaved) decoding protocol has been implemented: randomly selecting floor(r*n) positions in the sequence for ground-truth tokens while autoregressively sampling all other positions.

The embedding replacement experiment has been implemented: for each incorrect prediction, replacing it with probability r' with the token x'_i whose codebook embedding z^q'_i has the highest cosine similarity to the correct embedding z^q_i (excluding z^q_i itself).

The CKA score has been implemented as Equation 13: CKA(X,Y) = <K_c, L_c>_F / (||K_c||_F * ||L_c||_F), where K_c and L_c are centered Gram matrices of the generator features and tokenizer embeddings respectively.
The tied codebook embedding baseline has been implemented (Press & Wolf, 2016; Weber et al., 2024) for comparison in the embedding regularization ablation.

Multiple noise augmentation strategies have been implemented for ablation: fixed ratio (ε=0.25 and ε=0.5), uniform random ratio ε~U(0,0.5), linear annealing f(t)=1-t, and truncated linear annealing f(t)=max(0,1-4/3*t).
reAR has been implemented and applied to the TiTok tokenizer (AR-TiTok-b64 configuration: 261M params) for the generalization experiment.
reAR has been implemented and applied to the AliTok tokenizer (reAR-B-AliTok configuration: 177M params) for the generalization experiment.
reAR has been implemented and applied to VAR-d16 using the same training settings as the main experiments.
The exposure bias analysis experiments (perfect vs. imperfect context with varying CTR) have been executed successfully without errors.
The embedding unawareness analysis experiments (token replacement with varying replacement ratio r') have been executed successfully without errors.
Training and evaluation of reAR-S, reAR-B, and reAR-L on ImageNet-1K for 400 epochs have been executed successfully without errors.
Generalization experiments training reAR with TiTok (400 epochs) and AliTok (800 epochs) tokenizers have been executed successfully without errors.
Ablation studies for noise augmentation strategies (Table 3) and embedding regularization layer choices (Table 4) for reAR-S have been executed successfully without errors.
CKA analysis experiments comparing hidden feature alignment between vanilla AR and reAR across transformer layers have been executed successfully without errors.
VAR-d16 training with reAR has been executed successfully without errors and FID/IS evaluated.
Sampling throughput (images/sec) has been measured on a single A800 GPU with batch size 128 using KV-cache for reAR models.
The main results match Table 1: reAR-L achieves FID=1.86 (cfg=10.0) and IS=316.9 with 461M params, compared to the AR-L baseline FID=3.02 and IS=256.2.
The results match Table 1: reAR-S (FID=2.00, IS=295.7, 201M params) outperforms LlamaGen-XL (FID=2.34, IS=253.9, 1.4B params) using only 14% of the parameters.
The results match Table 1: reAR-B achieves FID=1.91 and IS=300.9 with 261M params.

The generalization results match Table 2: reAR-B-AliTok achieves FID=1.42 with 177M params, matching REPA (FID=1.42 with 675M params) and outperforming AR-AliTok-B (FID=1.50) and RAR-B-AliTok (FID=1.52).
The generalization results match Table 2: reAR-TiTok-b64 achieves FID=4.01 with 261M params, improving over AR-TiTok-b64 (FID=4.45) and RAR-TiTok-b64 (FID=4.07).
The scaling results match Figure 4: FID consistently decreases as model size increases (reAR-S → reAR-B → reAR-L) across all training steps.
The computation cost results match Table 5: reAR-B adds only ~0.03 min/epoch over AR-B (8.14 vs. 8.11 min), and reAR-L adds ~0.06 min/epoch over AR-L (16.05 vs. 15.99 min).

The noise ablation results match Table 3: fixed ε=0.5 causes training collapse (FID=3.15), truncated linear annealing f(t)=max(0,1-4/3t) achieves best FID=2.00, and noise-only (without embedding reg) gives FID=2.18.
The embedding regularization ablation results match Table 4: EN@0+DE@15 achieves the best FID=19.72 and IS=61.3, while tied codebook embedding (FID=21.08) barely improves over vanilla AR (FID=21.32).
The regularization layer analysis results match Table 4: EN@5+DE@15 (FID=21.36) performs worse than EN@0+DE@15 (FID=19.72), confirming that encoding regularization must be at the first layer.

The nearby regularization layer results match Table 7: DE@15 (FID=20.03) is the best among DE@13 through DE@17, with nearby layers yielding similar performance (e.g., DE@14: FID=20.17, DE@16: FID=20.11).

The exposure bias analysis results match Figure 3(a) and Figure 8(a-d): under the same CTR, imperfect context consistently yields higher LPIPS and lower PSNR than perfect context, demonstrating that exposure bias propagates structural errors in visual AR.

The embedding unawareness analysis results match Figure 3(b) and Figure 8(e-g): increasing the embedding replacement ratio r' consistently improves LPIPS and PSNR while keeping CTR unchanged, even though perplexity increases.

The CKA analysis results match Figure 9(b-c): reAR increases CKA similarity between generator features and encoded token embeddings at early layers (l=0) and between generator features and decoded (next-token) embeddings at deeper layers (~l=15).

The robustness analysis results match Figure 9(a): reAR achieves higher CTR than vanilla AR on unseen validation data (12.3 vs. 11.8) and on noisy unseen data (9.0 vs. 8.3) with 10% token replacement.
The VAR generalization results match Table 6: reAR applied to VAR-d16 improves FID from 3.55 to 3.39 and IS from 274.4 to 276.6 without tuning any hyperparameters.

The joint effect results match Table 3: combining noise augmentation (ε~U(0,f(t))) with embedding regularization achieves FID=2.00, which outperforms noise-only (FID=2.18) and embedding-reg-only (ε=0.0, FID=2.12), confirming the complementary nature of both components.
